coredata.dc:title: Integrating Pt nanoparticles with carbon nanodots to achieve robust cascade superoxide dismutase-catalase nanozyme for antioxidant therapy

coredata.dc:description: Nanozymes, nanomaterials with intrinsic enzyme mimetic activity, have emerged and been used in a broad range of applications yet are constrained with limited catalytic efficiency. Herein, by integrating carbon nanodots (CNDs) with platinum nanoparticles (PtNPs), a novel Pt@CNDs nanocomposite was engineered as an efficient nanozyme with superoxide dismutase (SOD)- and catalase (CAT)-like specific activities of 12,605 U/mg and 3172 U/mg, respectively. The PtNPs were deposited on the surface of CNDs by in situ chemical decomposition of Pt4+ to produce Pt@CNDs, in which CNDs served as a SOD mimic while PtNPs could mimic CAT, thereby forming a cascade antioxidant nanozyme system. Of note, the Pt@CNDs could significantly scavenge hydroxyl radicals as well. The carbonyl and hydroxyl groups of CNDs could bind with PtNPs, which promoted the electron transfer between PtNPs and CNDs that endowed the Pt@CNDs with excellent catalytic performance. Furthermore, Pt@CNDs could enter living cells and target mitochondria, thereby reducing the upregulated reactive oxygen species (ROS) level. Moreover, in vivo experimental results indicated that Pt@CNDs could effectively relieve ROS-induced inflammation in living mice. This work provides a promising strategy to design nanozymes with desired catalysis activity evidence by integrating CNDs and nanometals.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The characterization of Pt@CNDs nanozyme. (A) TEM images of CNDs, the scale bar = 10 nm. Inset: HR-TEM of CNDs, the scale bar = 2 nm. (B) TEM image of Pt@CNDs, the scale bar = 5 nm. (C) HR-TEM image of Pt@CNDs, the scale bar = 2 nm. (D) XRD patterns of CNDs, PtNPs, Pt@CNDs. (E-G) C 1 s high-resolution XPS spectrum with identification of peaks by curve fitting of CNDs (E), Re-CNDs (F), and Pt@CNDs (G). (H) Pt 4 f high-resolution XPS spectrum with identification of peaks by curve fitting of Pt@CNDs. (I) Zeta potential of CNDs and Pt@CNDs.

The cascade ROS scavenging activities of Pt@CNDs nanozyme. (A) The mixed solution L-methionine, riboflavin, and NBT in the absence and presence of CNDs and Pt@CNDs respectively in a concentration-dependent approach. (B) The ESR spectra of the mixed solution of xanthine, xanthine oxidase, and DMPO in the absence and presence of CNDs or Pt@CNDs with the concentration of 20 μg/mL. (C) The illustration of SOD-like activity of Re-CNDs and Pt@CNDs. (D) The H2O2 scavenging of CNDs, PtNPs, or Pt@CNDs with the concentration of 25 μg/mL by recording the absorbance of 240 nm in a time-driven mode. Inset: The photo of comparison of the mixed solution H2O2 in the absence and presence of CNDs, PtNPs, or Pt@CNDs with concentration of 25 μg/mL. (E)The CAT-like activities of PtNPs and Pt@CNDs were measured by monitoring the dissolved O2 at the same concentrations of 1.34 μg/mL. (F) typical kinetic curves of oxygen generation from the decomposition of H2O2 catalyzed by Pt@CNDs with concentrations of 0.17 μg/mL. (G)The •OH scavenging capacity of CNDs and Pt@CNDs, respectively, with the same concentration of 20 μg/mL detected by ESR. (H) the mixed solution Fenton reagent and MB in the absence and presence of CNDs or Pt@CNDs with the same concentration of 25 μg/mL. (I) Schematic presentation of cascade ROS scavenging activities of Pt@CNDs nanozyme.

Surface structure modification to study the mechanism of synergy effect between PtNPs and CNDs. (A) Illustration of CNDs modification. (B) The SOD-like activities of Pt@CNDs-A, Pt@CNDs-B, and Pt@Re-CNDs-A. (C) The CAT-like activities of Pt@CNDs-A, Pt@CNDs-B, and Pt@Re-CNDs-A. (D) FT-IR and (G) 1H NMR spectra of CNDs, CNDs-PS, and CNDs-A. (E) FT-IR spectra of CNDs-EDA and CNDs-B. (F) FT-IR spectra of Re-CNDs and Re-CNDs-A. (H) 1H NMR spectra of CNDs-B. (I) 1H NMR spectra of Re-CNDs and Re-CNDs-A.

Oxidation-damaged cell targeting ability and ROS scavenging ability of Pt@CNDs nanozyme. (A) Quantification of the flow cytometry result of Pt@CNDs-Cy5.5 accumulation in RAW264.7 cells in the absence or presence of H2O2. Typical fluorescence images (B) and quantification analysis (C and D) of the colocalization of the Pt@CNDs-Cy5.5 with mitochondria stained with Mito-Tracker. Intracellular SOD-like activity (E), CAT-like activity (F), and ROS scavenging capability (G) of Pt@CNDs, and their quantification of the flow cytometry results (H-K). The data are shown as mean± SD (n = 3). ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001, **** p < 0.0001, ns: not significant, T-test.

In vivo fluorescence imaging. (A) In vivo imaging, (B) the distribution in major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney), (C) average radiant efficiency at different time points after intravenous injection of Pt@CNDs-Cy5.5, and (D) half-life of Pt@CNDs-Cy5.5 in plasma. The data are shown as mean± SD (n = 3). ** p < 0.01, T-test.

The scale bar = 200 µm. (H) Blood biochemical analysis of different test groups. (I) Routine blood work of different test groups. The data are shown as mean± SD (n = 3). * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001, **** p < 0.0001, T-test.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanozymes, nanomaterials with intrinsic enzyme mimetic activity, have emerged and been used in a broad range of applications yet are constrained with limited catalytic efficiency. Herein, by integrating carbon nanodots (CNDs) with platinum nanoparticles (PtNPs), a novel Pt@CNDs nanocomposite was engineered as an efficient nanozyme with superoxide dismutase (SOD)- and catalase (CAT)-like specific activities of 12,605 U/mg and 3172 U/mg, respectively. The PtNPs were deposited on the surface of CNDs by in situ chemical decomposition of Pt4+ to produce Pt@CNDs, in which CNDs served as a SOD mimic while PtNPs could mimic CAT, thereby forming a cascade antioxidant nanozyme system. Of note, the Pt@CNDs could significantly scavenge hydroxyl radicals as well. The carbonyl and hydroxyl groups of CNDs could bind with PtNPs, which promoted the electron transfer between PtNPs and CNDs that endowed the Pt@CNDs with excellent catalytic performance. Furthermore, Pt@CNDs could enter living cells and target mitochondria, thereby reducing the upregulated reactive oxygen species (ROS) level. Moreover, in vivo experimental results indicated that Pt@CNDs could effectively relieve ROS-induced inflammation in living mice. This work provides a promising strategy to design nanozymes with desired catalysis activity evidence by integrating CNDs and nanometals.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The Pt@CNDs nanozyme is encoded with efficient cascade catalytic activity since the CNDs served as a SOD mimic while PtNPs could mimic catalase with the specific activities of 12,605 U/mg and 3172 U/mg, respectively. The in vitro and in vivo experiments indicated that the Pt@CNDs could scavenge intracellular ROS sufficiently to protect biosystems against ROS-induced damage.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanozymes, nanomaterials with intrinsic enzymatic-like activity, have attracted extensive attention recently. Benefit from the advantages of low cost, high stability, flexible operation, and easy large-scale production, nanozymes have been used in various applications from medical to biological to industrial [1–5]. Among them, the efficient nanozyme-catalyzed reactive oxygen species (ROS) scavenging has been used for relieving cellular oxidative stress and related antioxidant therapy [6]. ROS, such as superoxide anion (O2 •−), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), and hydroxyl radical (•OH), are products of aerobic metabolism in organisms and play a crucial role in cell signaling, homeostasis, and pathogen defense [7–10]. Once the up-regulated ROS exceeded the cellular antioxidant response, oxidative stress emerged and induced lipid peroxidation, protein denaturation, or DNA damage [11], triggering various diseases such as cancer, neuron degeneration, bone regeneration, cardiac protection, and inflammatory-related diseases, etc. [12,13]. Therefore, efficient nanozyme-mediated ROS scavenging is highly desirable in the treatment of ROS-related diseases.

Various nanozymes have been designed to defend the oxidative damage. For example, noble metal (Au [14], Pt [15], Pd [16], Rh [17], Ir [18], etc.) nanoparticles, metal oxide (CeO2 [19], RuO2 [20], Mn3O4 [21], Co3O4 [22], NiO [23], Cu5.4O [24], etc.) nanostructures, and carbon nanomaterials (C60 [25], hydrophilic carbon clusters [26], etc.) showed intrinsic catalase (CAT)-, superoxide dismutase (SOD)- or glutathione peroxidase (GPx)-like activities which can scavenge intracellular excess ROS, indicating their great potential in antiaging, neuroprotection, and alleviating inflammation, etc. For clinical applications, nanozymes are required to possess high ROS scavenging ability, robust stability, and good biocompatibility. Platinum nanoparticles (PtNPs) are potentially versatile nanozymes that can be used in a variety of biomedical applications. Fan et al. prepared PtNPs with a size of 1–2 nm by using an apoferritin protein shell as the nanoreactor. The Pt-Ft NPs exhibited both CAT- and peroxidase (POD)-like activities [27]. Similarly, Liu et al. reported PtNPs with CAT- and POD-like activities in neutral/alkaline and acidic conditions, respectively [28]. However, the undesirable catalytic activity and low colloidal stability of PtNPs remained the major challenges for their practical applications. Many efforts have been devoted to the construction of hybrid nanozymes for improving catalytic activity and stability [29–31]. It is demonstrated that coupling noble metal nanoparticles with carbon could achieve higher catalytic activity due to charge transfer at the metal−carbon interface and facilitated substrate adsorption [32].

Carbon nanodots (CNDs), fluorescent carbon nanoparticles with a size of less than 10 nm, are well known as excellent fluorescent probes due to their unique photoluminance properties, good biocompatibility, excellent water dispersity, and high stability [33–36]. Besides, the nanozyme properties of CNDs have gradually attracted attention. However, most of the research focused on the peroxidase-like activity and applications in the biochemical detection of CNDs [37,38]. Few studies reported the antioxidant nanozyme activity of CNDs [39,40]. Considering the large specific surface area and rich oxygen-containing groups of CNDs, it is feasible to integrate CNDs with metal nanoparticles. Previous works reported that the CNDs–Pt nanocomposites exhibited higher catalytic activities, including peroxidase-like, electrocatalytic, and photocatalytic activities than individual CNDs and PtNPs (details in Table S1) [41–43]. Similarly, Cobalt@CNDs, CNDs/Fe3O4, and CNDs/CuO nanocomposites also possessed higher peroxidase-like activity than CNDs and metal/metal oxide nanoparticles [44–46]. Whereas antioxidant nanozymes with a desirable catalytic effect based on CNDs still remain a great challenge.

Herein, the Pt@CNDs nanocomposite was constructed by integrating CNDs with PtNPs, which could be utilized as an excellent antioxidant nanozyme for protecting biosystems from ROS-induced damages ( Scheme 1). The as-achieved Pt@CNDs nanozyme is encoded with efficient cascade catalytic activity since the CNDs served as a SOD mimic to catalyze the dismutation of O2 •− to H2O2 while PtNPs could mimic catalase for catalyzing the decomposition of the producing H2O2 to water. The Pt@CNDs could also significantly remove hydroxyl radicals, as well as O2 •− and H2O2. Additionally, the carbonyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of CNDs play a key role in preventing PtNPs aggregation, endowing Pt@CNDs with good dispersibility and stability in physiological conditions. Importantly, we found that Pt@CNDs exhibited cell penetration and mitochondria targeting capability. Combining the enhanced catalytic activity of Pt@CNDs coming from the synergy effect between CNDs and PtNPs, the Pt@CNDs exhibited a remarkable therapeutic performance for in vivo inflammation induced by oxidative stress of mice, thus paving a promising avenue to design high-performance nanozymes for treating ROS-related diseases.

The CNDs were synthesized by oxidation of activated charcoal in the mixture of HNO3 and H2SO4 (details in SI). The TEM image of CNDs existed as monodispersed nanoparticles with a small size of ∼2 nm ( Fig. 1 A). PtNPs were deposited on the surface of CNDs by reducing Pt4+ using NaBH4, producing Pt@CNDs (details in SI). The deposited PtNPs exhibited a narrow size distribution with an average diameter of 3.2 ± 0.5 nm (Fig. 1 B). As shown in the HR-TEM image of Pt@CNDs (Fig. 1 C), the lattice of 0.23 nm corresponds to the (111) facet of Pt while the lattice of 0.21 nm could be ascribed to the (100) facet of graphite that was observed in CNDs. The crystallite nature of Pt@CNDs was identified by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD). The diffraction peaks with 2θ values ranging from 22° to 25°consisted with (002) facet of graphite [powder diffraction file (PDF Card No. 75–1621)], while the peaks with 2θ values of 39.9°, 46.1°, 68.1°, 81.5°, and 85.9° corresponded to (111), (200), (220), (311), and (222) facets of Pt, respectively [powder diffraction file (PDF Card No. 04–0802)] ( Fig. 1 D). The chemical composition and valence states of the elements were then analyzed by the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopies (XPS). The C 1 s high-resolution XPS spectra of CNDs, Re-CNDs (reduced by NaBH4) and Pt@CNDs could be well fitted by four peaks at 284.8, 286.0, 287.9, and 288.9 eV according to CC/C-C, C-O, CO, and COOH, respectively ( Fig. 1 E-G) . Meanwhile, the Pt 4 f XPS spectrum of Pt@CNDs exhibits two peaks at binding energies of Pt 4 f7/2 and Pt 4 f5/2, respectively (Fig. 1 H). The Pt 4 f7/2 peak was well fitted to two peaks at 71.2 and 72.4 eV, attributing to Pt0 (80%) and Pt2+ (20%), respectively. The EDX spectrum further confirmed that the elemental composition of Pt@CNDs was C, O, and Pt (Fig. S1). The zeta potential of CNDs was measured to be − 53.4 ± 0.8 mV, indicating their excellent stability (Fig. 1 I). In the absence of any ligands or coating molecules, the bare PtNPs were very unstable and coagulated to form aggregates (Fig. S2). With the assistance of CNDs, the Pt@CNDs exhibited a negative potential of − 28.1 ± 0.4 mV, indicating the high stability of Pt@CNDs (Fig. 1 I).

The formation of dispersive Pt@CNDs suggested most PtNPs grown on the surface of CNDs, while the PtNPs created in bulk solution could be removed during separation due to their poor dispersibility in solution. In the absence of CNDs, the black precipitate was obtained (Fig. S3). As the concentration of CNDs increased, the amount of precipitate gradually decreased. The dosage of CNDs in the synthesis has been optimized. When the dosage of CNDs was 0.8 mg, the Pt@CNDs exhibited optimal nanozyme activity and excellent stability (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The cascade nanozyme activity of Pt@CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: The SOD-­like activity of Pt@CNDs was evaluated by using nitrotetrazolium blue chloride (NBT) as the indicator of O2 •−. NBT could be specifically reduced by O2 •−, generating a product with an absorbance peak at 560 nm. The elimination efficiency of O2 •− of Pt@CNDs was dependent on their concentration, and the SOD-like activity of Pt@CNDs was higher than that of the CNDs ( Fig. 2 A). The SOD­-like activity of Pt@CNDs was also assessed by electron spin resonance (ESR) spectroscopy. O2 •− generated from the reaction of xanthine and xanthine oxidase could be trapped by 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO), producing the adduct of DMPO/•OOH. As shown in Fig. 2 B, the ESR signal of DMPO/•OOH decreased dramatically in the presence of Pt@CNDs or CNDs and the Pt@CNDs exhibited better O2 •− scavenging capability than CNDs, agreeing with the result of NBT method. Moreover, the SOD-like activity of CNDs was quantitatively tested to be ∼ 5000 U/mg by employing a commercial SOD assay kit (WST-1), while the PtNPs prepared without CNDs showed no obvious SOD-like activity, indicating that the SOD-like activity of Pt@CNDs originates from CNDs rather than PtNPs (Fig. S5). As shown in Fig. 2 C, the SOD-like specific activity of Pt@CNDs was calculated to be 12,605 U/mg according to the weight ratio (∼50%) of CNDs in Pt@CNDs, which was even 3-fold better than the natural SOD we tested[66] and was higher than the many previously reported nanomaterials ( Fig. 2 C and Table S2 ). Of note, after being reduced by NaBH4 (using the same process of Pt@CNDs synthesis, indicated as Re-CNDs), the Re-CNDs showed much lower SOD-like activity of 496 U/mg (Fig. 2 C). This was consistant with our lastest work about the catalytic mechanism of the SOD nanozyme [66], in which we revealed that the carbonyl groups of CNDs are the catalytic site of the SOD-like activity and the hydroxyl/carboxyl groups play an important role in binding with the superoxide anion. NaBH4 reduces the carbonyl groups on the surface of CNDs, which destroys the catalytic site of CNDs and thereby makes the Re-CNDs with a lower SOD-like activity. These results suggested that the SOD-like activity of CNDs in the nanocomposite was enhanced significantly.

H2O2, the product of the dismutation of O2 •−, has the highest stability and the highest intracellular concentration among the biologically relevant ROS [47]. So, it is essential to scavenge the H2O2 to protect organisms from oxidative damage. The CAT­-like activity of Pt@CNDs could be directly evaluated by observing the formation of bubbles, which comes from the catalytic decomposition of H2O2 to O2. As displayed in Fig. 2 D, many O2 bubbles were quickly generated in the H2O2 solution added with Pt@CNDs. Few O2 bubbles formed in the H2O2 solution containing PtNPs while no O2 bubbles were observed in the CNDs-H2O2 solution. Consistent with this, by monitoring the intensity of the characteristic absorption peak of H2O2 at 240 nm, the H2O2 consumption was detected to confirm that the Pt@CNDs showed higher CAT-like activity than PtNPs while CNDs could not catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 (Fig. 2 D). The CAT­-like activity of Pt@CNDs was further tested by monitoring the dissolved O2. As shown in Fig. 2 E, the oxygen concentration increased rapidly by 8.15 mg/L within 2 min, while the O2 concentration increased by 2.59 mg/L in the H2O2 solution containing PtNPs. It was notable to mention that the catalytic capacity was positively associated with the concentrations of Pt@CNDs and H2O2 (Fig. S6). The catalysis decomposition of H2O2 by Pt@CNDs followed typical Michaelis–Menten kinetics. The K M value of Pt@CNDs with H2O2 as the substrate was calculated to be 42.84 × 10−3 M while the V max value was 24.82 × 10−10 M·min−1 (Fig. 2 F). The CAT-like activity of Pt@CNDs was determined to be 556 U/mg according to their whole mass. Considering that CNDs possess no CAT-like activity, the CAT-like activity of Pt NPs in Pt@CNDs was calculated to be 3172 U/mg according to the weight ratio (∼17.5%) of Pt. The specific activity of the natural CAT enzyme was also evaluated by the same dissolved oxygen generation method to be ca. 2 × 105 U/mg ( Fig. S7A ), and the K M value of natural CAT for H2O2 was calculated to be 28.93 × 10−3 M and the V max value was 64.43 × 10−6 M·min−1 (Fig. S7B). Although the natural CAT catalyze the decomposition of H2O2 to produce oxygen more efficiently, its application is limited by the high manufacturing cost, complex production, poor stability and strict catalytic conditions. Compared with natural enzymes, nanozymes with low cost, high stability, strong durability, and large-scale production. As shown in Table S3, the K M value of Pt@CNDs was smaller than that of the many previously reported nanomaterials, indicating the Pt@CNDs have a better affinity for H2O2, possibly due to the small size of PtNPs in Pt@CNDs and the rich oxygen-containing groups on the surface of CNDs which could bind with H2O2 [48]. Meanwhile, the continuous catalytic oxygen production capacity of Pt@CNDs was also recorded by continuously adding H2O2 to the reaction system (details in SI). The results showed that Pt@CNDs could continuously maintain CAT-like activity for at least eight cycles (Fig. S8). Moreover, the POD-like activity of Pt@CNDs was investigated by using 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethyl-benzidine (TMB) and H2O2 as substrates. As shown in Fig. S9, Pt@CNDs showed the capability to oxidize TMB in a pH-dependent manner, with maximum activity at pH ∼ 5. When the pH increased to ∼7, the POD-like activity of Pt@CNDs became minimal. Therefore, Pt@CNDs mainly exhibited SOD- and CAT-like activities to eliminate ROS rather than the POD-like activity to generate ROS under physiological conditions.

In addition, the •OH scavenging activity of Pt@CNDs was also tested. The Fenton reaction between Fe2+ and H2O2 was employed to generate •OH. The ESR spectra of DMPO/•OH adducts were illustrated in Fig. 2 G. The signal of DMPO/•OH decreased significantly in the presence of Pt@CNDs with a concentration of 20 μg/mL, suggesting their excellent •OH scavenging capability. Methylene blue (MB) with an obvious absorption peak at 664 nm could be oxidized by •OH to produce a colorless product, which could indicate the concentration of •OH in the solution. As shown in Fig. 2 H, after mixed MB with Fe2+/H2O2, the absorption peak of MB almost disappeared, indicating the sufficient oxidation of MB. In contrast, the absorption intensity recovered by 80% in the presence of Pt@CNDs with a concentration of 25 μg/mL, suggesting 80% of the •OH was scavenged. Of note, no O2 bubble was observed in the above experiments, suggesting the •OH was scavenged by Pt@CNDs with a non-CAT-like pathway (Fig. S10). These results indicated the cascade SOD-CAT nanozyme activities of Pt@CNDs and their hydroxyl radical scavenging capacity as well (Fig. 2 I).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The mechanism of synergy effect between PtNPs and CNDs
ce:section.ce:para: Such high SOD- and CAT-like activities of Pt@CNDs encourage us to study the mechanism of synergy effect between PtNPs and CNDs. Kim et al. suggested that the carboxyl groups of nanodiamond could bind with Au3+, giving rise to the nucleation and growth of AuNPs [32]. Here, the CNDs have sp2 matrix with carboxyl, hydroxyl, and carbonyl groups on the surface [49]. Both carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of CNDs may coordinate with Pt4+ [50–52]. To obtain the direct evidence linking the specific functional groups with the coordination between Pt and CNDs, controllable surface modification was performed (details in SI).

To study the contribution of the hydroxyl on the surface of CNDs to the formation of Pt@CNDs, we selectively used 1, 3-propanesultone (PS) to deactivate carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. PS reacted with carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of CNDs to form ester and ether, respectively, which was demonstrated in our previous works [49,53]. The ester can hydrolyze under alkaline conditions while ethers cannot. After the hydrolysis of CNDs (reacted with PS) in 0.5 M sodium hydroxide solution, CNDs-A, in which only the hydroxyl groups were passivated, were obtained ( Fig. 3 A). As shown in Fig. 3 D, the characteristic absorption peaks of the sulfonic acid group (−SO3 −) at 528, 610, and 1047 cm−1 appeared in the FT IR spectrum of CNDs (reacted with PS) and decreased in that of CNDs-A, while the stretching vibration of the hydrogen bond in carboxyl group (2560 cm−1) decreased in the FT IR spectrum of CNDs (reacted with PS) and restored in that of CNDs-A. In the 1H NMR spectrum of CNDs (reacted with PS) (Fig. 3 G), the new absorption peaks appeared at 2.1, 2.9, and 4.4 ppm corresponding to 1H nuclei of β, α, and γ of −SO3 −, respectively. These characteristic absorption peaks decreased obviously in the 1H NMR spectrum of the CNDs-A. These results showed that the hydroxyl groups were passivated.

To study the contribution of the carboxyl on the surface of CNDs to the formation of Pt@CNDs, the carboxyl group on the surface of the CNDs was first converted into amide, and the amino group was exposed on the surface of CNDs. Then the aminated CNDs reacted with PS, in which the amino and the hydroxyl groups of the resulting CNDs (CNDs-B) were passivated (Fig. 3 A). As shown in Fig. 3 E, the stretching vibration of the hydrogen bond in the carboxyl group (2560 cm−1), the CO stretching vibration of the carboxyl group (1720 cm−1) and the O-H (3360 cm−1) stretching vibration of CNDs-EDA decreased obviously. A new characteristic absorption peak appeared at 3180 cm−1 attributing to the stretching vibration of N-H, and the peaks in the range of 2840–2970 cm−1 according to the absorption of C-H in aliphatic hydrocarbon indicated that carboxyl groups on the surface of CNDs were transformed to amide groups. In the FT-IR spectrum of CNDs-B, the characteristic absorption peaks of the sulfonic acid group at 528, 610, and 1047 cm−1 appeared, indicating the PS molecular was attached to the surface of CNDs. Moreover, the absorption of O-H and N-H decreased obviously while that of C-O-C and C-N-C (1160–1220 cm−1) increased, suggesting that the hydroxyl and amine have reacted with PS. In the 1H NMR spectrum of CNDs-B (Fig. 3 H), the new absorption peaks appeared at 2.1 and 2.9 ppm corresponding to 1H nuclei of β and α of −SO3 −, respectively. These results showed the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups were passivated.

To study the contribution of the carbonyl on the surface of CNDs to the formation of Pt@CNDs, sodium borohydride (NaBH4) was used to reduce the carbonyl to form hydroxyl on the surface of CNDs. The resulting Re-CNDs were reacted with PS and then underwent a hydrolysis process in 0.5 M sodium hydroxide solution. So, the hydroxyl on the surface of Re-CNDs was converted into the ether as mentioned above, producing Re-CNDs-A with less carbonyl content and passivated hydroxyl (Fig. 3 A). As shown in Fig. 3 F, compared with Re-CNDs, the characteristic absorption peaks of the −SO3 − at 528, 610, and 1047 cm−1 appeared in the FT IR spectrum of Re-CNDs-A. Meanwhile, in the 1H NMR spectrum of Re-CNDs, the peaks ranging from 3.38 to 3.74 ppm decreased, suggesting the carbonyl groups were reduced to hydroxyl groups. The peaks ranging from 1.05 to 2.00 ppm increased (Fig. 3 I), which can be attributed to the β-H of the newly formed hydroxyl group converted from the carbonyl group. In the 1H NMR spectrum of Re-CNDs-A, the new absorption peaks appeared at 2.1, and 2.9 ppm corresponding to 1H nuclei of β and α of −SO3 −, respectively (Fig. 3 I). These results showed that the carbonyl groups of CNDs were converted to hydroxyl groups, and then the hydroxyl groups were passivated.

When replacing CNDs in the synthesis with CNDs-A, CNDs-B and Re-CNDs-A with the optimal dosage, a large amount of precipitate (PtNPs aggregation) was observed (Fig. S11). Pt@CNDs-B, Pt@CNDs-A, and Pt@Re-CNDs-A exhibited lower nanozyme activities than that of Pt@CNDs (Fig. 3 B and C). Of note, the SOD- and CAT-like activities of Pt@CNDs-B were higher than those of Pt@CNDs-A and Pt@Re-CNDs-A. The precipitate of the resulting solution of Pt@CNDs-B was less than that of Pt@CNDs-A and Pt@Re-CNDs-A (Fig. S11). Meanwhile, the weight ratios of Pt in Pt@CNDs-B, Pt@CNDs-A, and Pt@Re-CNDs-A were calculated to be 12.03%, 4.42%, and 1.15%, respectively, by ICP-MS (Table S4), consistent to the above results. These results indicated that carbonyl and hydroxyl promoted the formation of Pt@CNDs, while carboxyl had the opposite effect. Therefore, we speculate that the carbonyl and hydroxyl groups of CNDs can coordinate with Pt4+, which facilitates the growing of PtNPs on the surface of CNDs and then produces Pt@CNDs. On the contrary, the carboxyl groups on the surface of the CNDs may be difficult to combine with Pt4+ due to electrostatic repulsion with PtCl6 2-, thus inhibiting the formation of PtNPs on CND surface. Moreover, it has been reported that the CO of polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) coordinated with Pt and Pd nanoparticles [54,55]. We also believe that the CO of CNDs could combine with PtNPs and play a crucial role in the catalytic reactions. The SOD-like activity of CNDs relies on the carbonyl groups coupling with the π- system (unpublished results). In the process of catalyzing the disproportionation of superoxide anion, electron transfer may occur between the PtNPs and the carbonyl of CNDs. Besides, PtNPs could mimic CAT to consume the H2O2 formed through the catalytic dismutation reaction of O2 •−. Thereby, the SOD enzyme activity of the CNDs was significantly improved. At the same time, PtNPs protected by CNDs in Pt@CNDs have a small size and larger specific surface area, which could expose more catalytic active sites. In addition, the carboxyl groups on the surface of CNDs have a strong binding ability to hydrogen peroxide. So, the CAT-like activity of PtNPs was also enhanced obviously.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Oxidation-damaged cell targeting ability and ROS scavenging ability of Pt@CNDs nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para: The excellent ROS scavenging activity makes Pt@CNDs promising for intracellular antioxidation. Methyl thiazolyl tetrazolium (MTT) assay was used to test the cytotoxicity of Pt@CNDs, in which RAW264.7 was exposed to Pt@CNDs with different concentrations. As depicted in Fig. S12, 95% of RAW264.7 cells restained viability after being exposed to the Pt@CNDs with a concentration up to 100 μg/mL for 24 h. The results also indicated that the Pt@CNDs showed relatively low cytotoxicity even if the exposure time was extended to 48 h. Moreover, Calcein-AM and propidium iodide (PI) co-staining were employed to verify the biosafety of Pt@CNDs (Fig. S13). These results suggest that the Pt@CNDs have relatively low toxicity for further biomedical applications.

Carbon-based nanomaterials have been shown to be able to cross the cell membrane in tumor cells with high ROS characteristics [56]. To assess whether Pt@CNDs were more prone to enter cells under a high level of ROS, we incubated Cy5.5-labeled Pt@CNDs (Pt@CNDs-Cy5.5) in RAW264.7 cells treated with different concentrations of H2O2. As shown in Fig. 4 A, compared to normal cells, the higher concentration of H2O2, the more Pt@CNDs-Cy5.5 was accumulated in cells. The results demonstrated that the Pt@CNDs could target oxidation-damaged cells. Mitochondria are the primary organelles for ROS production in living cells [57,58], thereby targeting mitochondria is crucial for Pt@CNDs to scavenge ROS [59]. The RAW264.7 cells were incubated with Pt@CNDs-Cy5.5, and mitochondria were stained with Mito-Tracker (green fluorescence). As displayed in Fig. 4 B, the red fluorescence of Pt@CNDs-Cy5.5 was detected and co-localized with the green signals from the stained mitochondria with a Pearson’s correlation coefficient of 0.91. The results demonstrated that the Pt@CNDs could target mitochondria (Fig. 4 C and D). The co-localization of Pt@CNDs with lysosomes was detected by Lyso-tracker Green. The fluorescence images showed that Pt@CNDs rarely colocalized with lysosomes ( Fig. S14A ). Additionally, the plot profile analysis showed that the red peaks (Pt@CNDs) and the green peaks (Lyso-tracker Green) were hardly parallel, suggesting almost no co-localization ( Fig. S14B ). The rarely colocalization of Pt@CNDs with lysosomes suggested that Pt@CNDs entered cells independent on membrane fusion or receptor-mediated endocytosis.

We further evaluate the intercellular SOD-like activity of Pt@CNDs by using dihydroethidium (DHE) as the O2 •− specific probe. Exogenously Rosup was used to stimulate the overproduction of intracellular ROS. As described in Fig. 4 E, the red fluorescence of oxidized DHE increased significantly in RAW264.7 cells after Rosup treatment. With the assistance of Pt@CNDs, the red fluorescence decreased obviously in a dose-dependent manner, indicating the Pt@CNDs could mimic SOD to eliminate O2 •− in cells. Using flow cytometry, we were able to quantify the intensity changes of DHE after different treatment groups. Fig. 4 I showed that cells co-incubated with Rosup and Pt@CNDs fluorescence were significantly lower. Similarly, the intercellular CAT-like activity of Pt@CNDs has also been confirmed by using [Ru(dpp)3] Cl2 (RDPP), a red oxygen-specific probe. Fig. 4 F showed that red fluorescence decreased significantly from RDPP quenched by oxygen in RAW264.7 cells incubated with Pt@CNDs, indicating the internalized Pt@CNDs can effectively catalyze the decomposition of intracellular H2O2 to produce O2. The flow cytometry analysis also showed that co-incubation with Pt@CNDs could decrease the fluorescence intensity of RDPP caused by the Rosup (Fig. 4 J).

The total intracellular ROS, including O2 •−, •OH, and H2O2, were detected by using a universal fluorescent probe, i.e., 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA), which could be hydrolyzed by intracellular esterase to produce DCFH. The probe could be easily loaded into cells because DCFH could not penetrate cell membranes. The intracellular ROS could oxidize DCFH to produce fluorescent DCF. So, the fluorescence intensity of DCF reflects the level of intracellular ROS. As displayed in Fig. 4 G, the fluorescence signal was negligible in the untreated or Pt@CNDs treated RAW264.7 cells, confirming their excellent biocompatibility. The strong fluorescence was observed in the cells treated by Rosup, while the fluorescence decreased obviously in the cells pretreated with Pt@CNDs, demonstrating the excellent ROS scavenging capability of Pt@CNDs. Furthermore, the ROS generation induced by Rosup and the ROS scavenging by the Pt@CNDs were quantitatively analyzed by flow cytometry. As shown in Fig. 4 H and K, more than 80% of ROS could be eliminated by the Pt@CNDs with a concentration of 50 μg/mL. Additionally, Fig. 4 L showed that the Pt@CNDs exhibited a desirable ability to protect RAW264.7 cells from H2O2 damage. Besides, the Pt@CNDs could also down-regulate pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, and IL-12), as described in Fig. S15. The results indicated that the Pt@CNDs could scavenge ROS under physiological conditions effectively and protect cells from oxidative stress damage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo protective effect of Pt@CNDs nanozyme against liver injury
ce:section.ce:para:
The intracellular antioxidant capability of Pt@CNDs makes them a promising candidate for treating ROS-induced inflammation in vivo. We first evaluated the pharmacokinetic behavior and the in vivo distribution in major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney) of Pt@CNDs nanozyme. It was found that most Pt@CNDs-Cy5.5 distributed in liver after 3 h intravenous injection, while Pt@CNDs-Cy5.5 mainly accumulated in the kidney at 6 h post-injection (
Fig. 5 A and B). Meanwhile, the statistical data of average radiant efficiency also showed that the highest Pt@CNDs-Cy5.5 accumulated in liver at 3 h and in kidney at 6 h (Fig. 5 C). These results indicated that Pt@CNDs could accumulate in the liver and might be metabolized by the kidneys. After Pt@CNDs-Cy5.5 were injected into normal mice via the tail veins, the blood was collected at different times. The half-life of Pt@CNDs-Cy5.5 in the plasma was also calculated to reach 48 min (Fig. 5 D). Compared with the normal group, the signal intensities were more obvious in the CCl4-treating group (Fig. 5 E-G). Such finding indicates that Pt@CNDs are promising candidates to protect the liver from ROS-induced injury.

Acute liver injury (ALI) is characterized by a rapid loss of hepatocyte functions with high mortality [60]. The excess generation of ROS in the liver during ALI has been considered a major cause of liver failure [61]. Recently, nanozymes with ROS scavenging activities have been widely applied in ALI regulation [62–64]. To investigate the protective effect of Pt@CNDs in vivo, an ALI mice model was constructed via intraperitoneal injection of CCl4. The mice were pre-treated with Pt@CNDs by intravenous injection 3 h before the CCl4 treatment, and the injury severity of the liver was evaluated after 24 h ( Fig. 6 A). The levels of serum aminotransferases, such as aspartate aminotransferase (AST) and alanine aminotransferase (ALT), are commonly used as biochemical markers for determining the occurrence of ALI. As shown in Fig. 6 B and C, CCl4-induced mice showed a sharp increase in both ALT and AST levels, indicating the successful establishment of the ALI mice model. Once pre-treated with Pt@CNDs, the ALT and AST levels of mice were significantly reduced. Besides, the decreased liver necrotic areas as indicated by the hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining further proved that Pt@CNDs can efficiently protect the liver from CCl4-induced hepatotoxicity (Fig. 6 D). Meanwhile, ALI mice pre-treated with Pt@CNDs exhibited a similar liver weight ratio to that of healthy mice, while PBS groups underwent a dramatic liver weight ratio increase (Fig. 6 E).

To further demonstrate the therapeutic efficacy of Pt@CNDs acting as a ROS scavenger in vivo, the activities of antioxidant enzyme (SOD, CAT, and GPx) in liver homogenate were also evaluated. As shown in Fig. 6 F-H, compared with CCl4 groups, the levels of antioxidant enzyme in ALI mice pre-treated by Pt@CNDs were significantly increased, reaching the levels of normal mice (PBS group). Meanwhile, pretreatment with Pt@CNDs could gradually attenuate the MDA level, further implying the antioxidant effect of Pt@CNDs (Fig. 6 I). We next evaluated the ROS level in liver tissues by DHE staining. As displayed in Fig. 6 J, excess ROS generation in the livers of ALI mice was dramatically inhibited after pretreatment with Pt@CNDs, which verified the robust antioxidant defense capability of Pt@CNDs in ALI treatment. Besides, anti-oxidative stress genes (Gpx1, HO-1) were up-regulated, and pro-oxidative stress genes (Nox2, Cyp2e1) were down-regulated in the liver of mice pre-treated with Pt@CNDs by using quantitative reverse transcription-polymerase chain reaction (qRT-PCR) (Fig. 6 L-O). Subsequently, the levels of inflammatory factors (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, and IL-12) in liver tissues were measured by qRT-PCR. As shown in Fig. 6 P-S, compared to the CCl4 group, the levels of these inflammatory factors in the liver of mice with pre-treatment of Pt@CNDs decreased significantly, confirming that the Pt@CNDs could mitigate inflammatory responses. Myeloperoxidase (MPO) is a marker of neutrophil recruitment to inflamed sites [65]. The liver MPO activity in Pt@CNDs-treated ALI mice was similar to that of healthy mice, whereas a significant rising was observed in CCl4 groups (Fig. 6 K), indicating the inflammatory regulation effect of Pt@CNDs. Taken together, these results demonstrated that Pt@CNDs have a prominent protective effect against ALI in vivo.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo protective effect of Pt@CNDs nanozyme against PMA-induced ear inflammation
ce:section.ce:para: Apart from ALI, hence, an ear inflammation model also was established to evaluate the anti-inflammation activity of Pt@CNDs in vivo. As shown in Fig. S16, the right ear of the experimental mouse exhibited rea and swollen after the treatment of phorbol 12-myristate 13-acetate (PMA) for 6 h, showing the success of the inflammation model. To verify the ROS scavenging capability of Pt@CNDs in vivo, the inflamed ears were thereafter subcutaneously treated with Pt@CNDs at the dose of 2.5 μg/kg and 5 μg/kg for each mouse, respectively (details in SI). As displayed in Fig. 7 A, no significant fluorescence was observed from mice treated with DHE only. In contrast, strong fluorescence from the inflamed ear was observed after treatment with PMA/DHE, indicating that PMA induced an elevated level of ROS. The fluorescence signals decreased significantly in the Pt@CNDs-treated inflamed ears (Fig. 7 B). Additionally, the histologic section of the ear treated with PMA showed obvious edema and neutrophil infiltration, compared with the control group or that treatment with Pt@CNDs only (Fig. 7 C). After treatment with Pt@CNDs, the swelling symptom and inflammatory cell infiltration levels were alleviated in a dose-dependent manner. Moreover, the levels of several inflammatory factors (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, and IL-12) in ear tissues were quantified by using qRT-PCR to further evaluate the therapeutic effect of Pt@CNDs on ear inflammation (Fig. 7 D-G). Meanwhile, H&E staining results of the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney of mice demonstrated no pathological changes in major organs, indicating good biocompatibility of Pt@CNDs (Fig. S17). The results of blood routine and biochemical analysis showed that both the control and treatment groups were at normal levels (Fig. 7 H and I). All of these results demonstrated that the Pt@CNDs did not cause significant side effects at the tested concentration. The above results demonstrated that the Pt@CNDs possessed efficient ROS scavenging capacity toward ear inflammation in live mice.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo biosafety analysis of Pt@CNDs nanozyme
ce:section.ce:para: Given their excellent efficacy for in vivo therapy, we then explored the biocompatibility and systemic toxicity of Pt@CNDs in healthy mice. As shown in Fig. 8 A and C, the blood biochemical analysis did not show any differences between Pt@CNDs-treated groups and the control groups in the levels of alanine transaminase (ALT), aspartate transaminase (AST), blood urea nitrogen (UREA), creatinine (CREA), creatine kinase (CK), and lactate dehydrogenase 1 (LDH 1) of mice after 10 and 30 days, indicating Pt@CNDs had minimal effect on the kidney and liver of the mice. Moreover, hematology indicators (white cell count, hemoglobin, etc.) of healthy mice with an injection of Pt@CNDs on 10th and 30th day were not significantly different from the PBS administered group (Fig. 8 B and D). Compared to the control group, the H&E staining of major organs, including the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney, suggested no obvious necrosis, congestion, and hemorrhage in the Pt@CNDs-treated group, demonstrating the good biocompatibility of Pt@CNDs (Fig. 8 E). These results show that the Pt@CNDs are biocompatible and tolerated at the therapeutic dose in healthy mice.

In summary, we designed a novel Pt@CNDs nanocomposite with SOD- and CAT-like activities by integrating carbon nanodots with platinum nanoparticles. The Pt@CNDs exhibited much higher catalytic activities compared with individual PtNPs or CNDs due to the synergistic effects between PtNPs and CNDs. In addition to the electron transfer between PtNPs and CNDs, the PtNPs could catalyze the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide, which was the product of the disproportionation of O2 •− catalyzed by CNDs, thereby increasing the SOD-like activity of CNDs. On the other hand, the PtNPs of Pt@CNDs were deposited on the surface of CNDs, small size without any coating molecules made the PtNPs more catalytic active sites and then enhanced the CAT-like activity. Moreover, the Pt@CNDs could also significantly scavenge hydroxyl radical as well as O2 •− and H2O2. Both in vitro and in vivo experiments indicated that the Pt@CNDs could scavenge intracellular ROS sufficiently to protect the biosystem against ROS-induced damage, demonstrating the great potential of Pt@CNDs as a new therapeutic candidate for the treatment of ROS-related diseases. This study also provides a promising strategy to design nanozymes with desired catalytic activity.

Y.Z., W.G., M.Z., and C.L. designed, conducted the experiments, and wrote the manuscript. Y.M., L.Z., Q.L., and K.T. provided technical and material support and helped with the investigations. M.Z., and C.L. supervised and supported this study and critically revised the manuscript.
